KEY WORDS AND PHRASES: axiomatic method, theory of programming'
proofs of programs, formal language definition, programming language
design, machine-independent programming, program documentation
CR CATEGORY: 4.0, 4.21,4.22, 5.20, 5.21,5.23, 5.24

A9

✓

In this paper an attempt is made to explore the logical foundations of computer programming by use of techniques which
were first applied in the study of geometry and have later
been extended to other branches of mathematics. This involves the elucidation of sets of axioms and rules of inference
which can be used in proofs of the properties of computer
programs. Examples are given of such axioms and rules, and
a formal proof o f a simple theorem is displayed. Finally, it is
argued that important advantages, both theoretical and practical, may follow from a pursuance of these topics.

===== slide 45 =====
Validity
A LCL triple ⊢A [P] c [Q] is valid if Q ⊆ [[c]]P ⊆ A(Q)
Is ⊢Int [x > 0] x := 10x [x ≥ 10] valid?
Is ⊢Int [x > 0,y ≥ 0] x := yx [x ≥ 0] valid?
Is ⊢Sign [x > 0,y > 0] x := yx [x = 42,y = 7] valid?
⋆

Is ⊢Sign [x > 0] (x := x + 1)

[x > 0] valid?

===== slide 46 =====
Logical correctness
Th.
#
If ⊢A [P] c [Q] then Q ⊆ [[c]]P ⊆ A(Q) = [[c]]A A(P)
Proof.
By induction on the derivation.

===== slide 48 =====
Logical completeness
Th.
If A is complete for any atomic command in c,
then any valid triple ⊢A [P] c [Q] can be derived
Proof.
We first derive ⊢A [P] c [[[c]]P],
then use [relax] with Q ⊆ [[c]]P

===== slide 49 =====
Intrinsic logical incompleteness
Th.
For any Turing complete language and any non-trivial
abstraction A, there are valid triples that cannot be proved
Proof.
See full version of LICS 2001 paper in JACM

Exercises

===== slide 53 =====
Validity
Which LCL triples are valid for any c and P ?

⊢{⊤} [P] c [false]
⊢{⊤} [P] c [true]
⊢Sign [x > 10] c [false]
⊢{⊤} [wlp(r, P)] c [P]

⊢{⊤} [P] c [false]
⊢{⊤} [P] c [true]
⊢Sign [x > 10] c [false]
⊢{⊤} [wlp(r, P)] c [P]

===== slide 57 =====
Soundness
Are these “mixed” HL+LCL inference rules valid ?

⊢A [P] c [Q]
{P} c {A(Q)}

⊢A [P] c [Q]
{A(P)} c {A(Q)}

===== slide 58 =====
Soundness
Are these “mixed” HL+LCL inference rules valid ?

⊢A [P] c [Q]
{P} c {A(Q)}

⊢A [P] c [Q]
{A(P)} c {A(Q)}

If ⊢A [P] c [Q] then [[c]]P ⊆ A(Q), hence {P} c {A(Q)} is valid
#

If ⊢A [P] c [Q] then [[c]]A(P) ⊆ [[c]] A(P) = A(Q), hence
{A(P)} c {A(Q)} is valid

===== slide 59 =====
Unsoundness 1
Prove that [conj] is unsound for LCL

⊢A[P1] c [Q1] ⊢A[P2] c [Q2]
⊢A [P1 ∧ P2] c [Q1 ∧ Q2]

[conj]

===== slide 60 =====
Unsoundness 1
Prove that [conj] is unsound for LCL

⊢A[P1] c [Q1] ⊢A[P2] c [Q2]
⊢A [P1 ∧ P2] c [Q1 ∧ Q2]

[conj]

Consider ⊢{⊤} [x = 0] x := 1 [x = 1]
and ⊢{⊤} [x = 1] x := 1 [x = 1]

By rule [conj] we could derive ⊢{⊤} [false] x := 1 [x = 1]
which is not sound!

===== slide 61 =====
Unsoundness 2
Show that the following rule is not sound

⊢A [P] x := nondet() [P[v/x]]
arbitrary
value

===== slide 62 =====
Unsoundness 2
Show that the following rule is not sound

⊢A [P] x := nondet() [P[v/x]]
arbitrary
value

Consider ⊢Id [x = 0] x := nondet( ) [1 = 0] ≡ [false]
which is not sound because Id(false) = ⊥ and

Id([[x := nondet( )]](x = 0)) = ⊤

===== slide 63 =====
Take home message
In theory, we prove
correctness.
In practice, we approximate
our own mistakes.
